speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 6, 1996. I'm interviewing Sydney Schwimmer. This is tape number three. Would you continue with what you were talking about at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So, I find myself here, during the month of April, May, June, July, in Brunn in protectorate, and what to do? Don't know where to do, what to do. But it came time that I've seen what's going on there, how it is almost impossible for Jews to get out. Yes, there  were some Jews who were very-- they're wealthy and French, and maybe they bought some visas to Cuba, to-- to Venezuela, and for some larger money, somehow, they will be able to get a exit visa. But a plain man like me couldn't get an exit visa. And couldn't get a visa. No place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm talking to myself. We have a few friends, the same position, the same situation there as I am. I can name their names. There is not too much food to eat, once a day, not where to earn money.

speaker: interviewee
text: At one point, I went-- somehow contractor gave me some job. For a week, I was working at a-- at a-- what you call it-- at a construction job. But after a week, they found out that the Jews, they didn't allow, so they fired me, but I still had a little money left from this. So here is my situation that no matter what I'm doing, I couldn't do nothing. To get out from there, you need an exit visa. Lines are standing there in the hundreds every day. Not Jews-- a lot SS, these are the one. Commanders were giving out an exit visa [INAUDIBLE] to be able to get out from protectorate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a passport. One day, I decide I also go there and try. What's-- what's going to happen? Otherwise I cannot get out from here. I already decided that here I cannot stay. There's no place for me. It's impossible to emigrate. I have no chance to see it. So maybe I'll go back to Hungary. From Hungary, I'll have more chance. I'll go to Romania or maybe I'll wait there until United States will give me a visa. They'll transfer my papers from Prague. So everything is planned.

speaker: interviewee
text: But how it is, exit visa for Jews is almost impossible, especially for me. So I go there one day. Line comes up. They see a Jew. They chase me away from there. Finally came to a-- to a situation. It was around in August, month August. I go into the Gestapo, they called it. And line came, I'm in, I'm going with my passport, because on a passport they have to have-- they give a visa exit from Czechoslovakia. And I declare that I'm from Hungary, I want to go back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came into the first visit to the first officer there, and he looks at me, and puts on a stamp and it's exit visa. Oh, and I was very glad. Here I'm at last had a visa. I was-- it was a nice man, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he sends me in-- in the second room where another officer has to sign it. I come in the other office, takes a look, Schwimmer, Zoltan, and he takes a big stamp and put on the passport on the stamp, Jude. Another with no exit visa, but Jude means Jew. They usually used to put in for Jews with a-- with a German stamp, Jude. Now, Jude-- and we forget about exit visa, and gave me some curse. I was glad getting out from there. I'm not going to tell details. I'm coming out, no visa. But in the passport is already written, Jude. You cannot take it out from the passport. I would [INAUDIBLE] the passport without Jude-- without Jude, somehow, I don't know. We will think of something else. But here is already, with this passport, I can do nothing-- as far as to get out. One day, I decided finally, I cannot stay here, I'm going back to Hungary. I'm going to back Hungary, I'll go on the train, and what's going to happen is going to happen. I had another friend by the name of Dr. Goldstein. He just did get his doctorate in medicine before Hitler came in. We're going, coming. Finally, one train, another train. We're coming to the border, smuggled in ourselves. We're going to Slovakia. We came to Bratislava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Bratislava's already good. We're going there to the Jewish community, getting some meals, some advice. And they start to say to us also, you're going back to Hungary. What are you going to Hungary? Finally we decided to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming back to Hungary to a border, a certain border-- the police, the Germans, the Slovakian, let us out, and the Hungarians come and chase us down from the train. You are not-- you are Jews. Chase down from the train in a field. Well, the train was stopped at the border. Don't know what to do. I'm going to be in a field. You cannot come into Hungary, you don't-- you don't have a entry visa. You don't have-- you are not a citizen. Despite we show we are from Dovhoje. Ah, it doesn't mean nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we wait. Comes another train. The police are again-- those-- these are the border, they're the custom borders.

speaker: interviewee
text: To make a long story short, we smuggle in ourselves. We going-- coming to Hungary. So we are in Hungary already. Coming back, I'm coming back to my father, to Dovhoje. Surprise-- I'm here. They knew already because I stopped in Beregsaz to my sister, and my friend other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are there. What I'm going to do there? I don't know nothing what's in Hungary. Now, your brothers are still at home in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother-- one was married. My sister was married. Only one of my sister is at home. They're not home. And they are-- my brother is in Hungary in there. And he is in the Hungarian army, my younger brother. My older brother is at home, in his home, but they don't live in Dovhoje. My older sister is married. She lives in Baragsaz. This was-- this was around August, end of August. And my father also tells me that these Hungarians who came in here now are different from the one I remember in World War I. They're very hard, the gendarmes. There's rules. They are not good to Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you came home, it's good, but what are you going to do? I'll try, maybe, to go someplace, see what's going on here. Now, was your father still able to maintain his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father-- this is-- this is in '39 in August, end of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally comes on a Friday, 1939, September 1, the war brokes out with Poland. Oh, this and that. So finally, you know the story, after three weeks Poland was defeated. We are staying there. We are reading the Hungarian papers. What's in the Hungarian papers? Only everything what they want to write. They are allies with Germany. But the Hungarians are here a complete different Hungarians. Let me give you a little bit-- I don't mean to give lessons here. But in our town in Dovhoje, the smaller towns, the Hungarian did send in gendarmes, police. What do you call it? Gendarmes. And who were there the gendarmes? Illiterate people. Maybe they had four grades top. And they're soldiers someplace, and now they did get this job. Maybe the commander wasn't an officer. The commander was maybe a-- who knows? Maybe he had a little more schooling, not too much. And these are illiterate, very, but they don't know they're illiterate. And they had took the power and they are interpreting the law in their own way and their own version.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there are a few collaborators in town. The Czechs moved away-- no Czechs. In Dovhoje by itself, were not too many Hungarians, but those who consider themselves Hungarians started to collaborate. So what everything is still all right, but, you know, you got to obey them and you are afraid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is their thing? They are beating up people, plainly. There's no courts. They're  beating up non-Jews, also. Anyone, they will find on the street something they don't like it to their liking, they beat them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But beating up is not just like we are thinking. Beating them up and all kind. And they are beating up especially Jews, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's right in the beginning. I can tell you stories, stories which I'm going to eliminate. Everyone who went through this Hungarian gendarmes-- I'm not talking in-- in every case, but in general, these were very primitive. You can't talk to them, you couldn't-- they couldn't write, they couldn't read. Very poorly. But they consider themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were chosen by the Hungarians and in  a sense that to give them jobs, so they became gendarmes, police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I find myself in this situation. What I'm going to do here? I spend the winter there. It's very difficult in Dovhoje. What I'm going to do there? I help out my father, and my father still has a little work. So far everything goes all right. So I-- at one point, they started to draft Jews. Was in the beginning of 1940. Jews to labor camps. No one understood what it means, a labor camp. They start to draft. And what is drafting? They send out a [NON-ENGLISH], they call it, a draft by mail or sometimes, they announce it. And you have to come to a certain place near your town, far from your town. And you go there, you find yourself among other Jews, and they give you a certain band, yellow band here, saying that we are Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in a group, and they used you for cleaning streets. They're harsh to you. And this is under the military rule, and they call it auxiliary military labor camps. That's how it started. I see myself, this situation is not good for me. I'll stay here, I'll getting a draft too. I'll move to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was everyone-- was every Jew in Dovhoje wearing a band, an arm band? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no no, only those who were drafted to this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were getting, were getting. They give that food also, and they sleep in private house. And unorganized in the beginning. Unorganized. Auxiliary labor camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I moved to Budapest. A long story. Where I'm going to go to work. I still don't have too much money, I don't have means, but something will happen in Budapest. I go to Budapest. Where do I go? Go in Jewish headquarters. Ah, there are certain cities where Jews lived together, like Rumbach, [NON-ENGLISH], and so on. I cannot enumerate. I speak Hungarian language. What I'm going to do from here? I'll start to see how to emigrate. From a small town, I cannot do nothing, but here there are organizations. There are the American consul. In the meantime, I left my papers to transfer from Prague, which I have an affidavit, to Budapest. And yeah, they transferred that to an account to the consul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they have the papers, but wait for the quota. So at least I have some number here to wait. It's a [INAUDIBLE]. Had a paper in the American consul-- well, my line will come and my--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no work, nothing in Budapest. So in this, why do I go to the Jewish headquarters? I mean, to go for me to a hotel, I have no money. But there are places. There is a woman has an apartment of two or three rooms. In one room she has-- what do you call? Occupants, sleepers. So she has six, eight beds and so she rents you a bed for a nominal sum. And it's not so bad what-- and to eat, there's also-- there are places, there are cheap places. So I find myself also a job there in the Jewish hospital, as a worker, not as a doctor. I, myself, and work a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: But here comes the story. 1940, as the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Germans occupied [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland, many Polish Jews fled to Hungary. Many fled to the Soviet Union. As you know, they are divided. I'm not going to tell you about story. But many fled there, Polish Jews. Also, they came in different place, different towns. Here in Budapest, in those places, there are-- it was around May-- May, June, 1940. There are raids at night, very many raids, and this-- where Jews live in this place, and they're looking for spies. And there is such a thing in Hungary, the [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the, what you call it, the-- the anti-espionage division.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is a spy here? I'm a spy. You're a spy. No one is a spy here. When they figured out the Jews who are coming from the Carpathian, or the Russian, or that these Jews, especially, are spies. And what do they do? They come at night there, and they look for identification, and they see if someone is not from Budapest and the Carpathian. Because if they are Carpathian Jew, youngster, that he's also a spy. So people are avoiding at night. They're coming home. And they're taking away the people to the headquarters at night, 1, 2 o'clock, down to these espionage police. In the morning they let them out. What are you going to do with them? They're not spies. But these were very Hungarian who-- they-- they understood their-- their job is looking for spies. And where are you looking for spies?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Jewish headquarters, and the Rumbach eventually. I live on Rumbach Street number 10. Especially houses and the towns are living there Jews. And you can mingle in the city, but over there you sleep at night, they know they're Jews. So me they didn't take. One day, so they didn't take my identification, I speak Hungarian. But they were not-- they were-- they were looking. And this was going on constantly, one street, another. [INAUDIBLE] Now, to go, for me, to live someplace else, I didn't have the means, I didn't have the money. And besides, you cannot go to live there, you see. When you come in a city in Budapest, you must go first to the police and have a registration where you're going to be, where you're going to live. And the lady from the house has to sign this that she accepts you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have such a registration, also. It's not like here, you come and you sleep. The registration-- and this registration remains in the police, and they have your name, your address, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, one night, they come in, they caught me, too. For no reason. But they caught too many. They usually used to take away not far from our street there. But the street was already full. The house, the police were already full, and we come there, there's no more room. What are they going? They take us away, Hungarian espionage. Of course, not uniform, just like-- like secret police. You cannot describe them. You are not an espionage-- [LAUGHS] or are you a spy? They came to Budapest, many like me, to look for some work, to look for something, because in Dovhoje and those small place they can do nothing. So I find myself here in Budapest, and so they took me also to where they are taken. They're taking me to-- taking us to a place, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is known like a bazaar where all the swindles are going down. It still exists now in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very big place. It's a marketplace. Call it a bazaar. You'll find all crooks there. You'll find all who are look-- who will steal away your purse. This  was going on for years. This is not a new thing in Budapest. And they put us-- and over there, years ago, they used to bring horses over to trade. And they had some-- some like little places where they would put the horse there and have him there chained up. And they have these chains there, so they chain us up with the right foot there and put the chain on us, not to escape, because they don't know what to do at night with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were maybe 10, 15, 20. They were doing all night. And in the morning they come, because they're not going to stay and watch us the whole night there. It was around 12, 1 o'clock. So I'm staying there. What can you do, staying chained up with a chain? So in the morning they come and they-- they bring you to the police. And the police, from where are you? And they start to tell you, if you once more come to Budapest, we are going to put you in jail. They didn't know what to do with us at the-- you come before the-- you didn't even come, the whole group came. So go home, but don't come anymore to Budapest. So it's a danger to live there. So for me, no place where to live. So I started to think, what to do from here? Where to go? And there's nothing, no explanation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have money to go to a fancier place. I got to live only in the Jewish quarter, where there is cheap. You can get something for $1 a night or even cheaper, something like this. In the meantime, comes up a story in Budapest that all Jews age 20, 40, or 45 have to register in the police to be drafted in labor camps. This was already before in Dovhoje, but now comes more stricter orders about these things. And I fall in also in that category.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, let me tell you something like this. In Hungary, there was constantly about written in the paper about the Jewish question, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN], I'm speaking Hungarian-- Jewish question. They were constantly busy what the Jewish question is, what to do with Jews now? And this Jewish question was then separated in three stages. Well, as the first is, who is a Jew? In Hungary were a lot of assimilated Jews. So not everyone is a Jew who  goes on the street. Maybe his mother, his grandfather, his grandmother. So they're first in the first stages in the first three stages.

speaker: interviewee
text: First stage, a Jew is defined and so on, this and this. The second stage is more harsh than the third stage. I have in front here, my book here, I have all this information that chronicles exactly about their laws, who's a Jew, the first  stage, the second stage, and the third stage. But here, certainly, not every Jew falls in. Someone who doesn't keep his religion, someone who wasn't circumcised, some was his father wasn't Jewish. And in-- in Budapest, 300,000 Jews lived there. Among those more are assimilated, they're more-- more-- are not considered the Jews-- he is a Jew only by--- by relation, but he's not a Jew. Yes, these are stories which I can write and read books about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, but I fall in this category. And you have to go to there to-- to register this, to draft. Number one, you're threatened that you're being killed, you're being in jailed, you're being this. And secondly, if your status is not registered, you cannot go to live no place. Another was, no one accepts you. And so when you want to come to live anyplace, you have to go to the police, and the police ask you, what's your military status? You have to write that you are drafted. You have to prove. So I figure, myself, so I'm going to take chances. I want to stay in Budapest, so I'm going to go over to that office and draft. I see there are many drafted. What's drafting? They put a line, maybe 100. Not everyone went, but I went, because I was a stranger and I couldn't stay. I couldn't go to live no place else without having this-- this status might clarify.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I come there. There's a line. You undress yourself. 100, maybe 200, going one, two, three. A doctor or an officer comes there. Well, anyone who's a cripple doesn't have-- there's no food. He's taken out and the rest are all drafted. And they give you a paper, drafted. Now, drafted doesn't mean nothing. You can work. So I'm drafted there. Already I can go anyplace I want. I have my status. I can register. Forgot to tell you about, I couldn't get my domicile in Dovhoje, my-- because I came late, so I'm not-- I'm not a citizen according to their interpretation. And so I'm struggling in Budapest. And every day is a different story. Still working on emigration, but still I cannot emigrate. There's no place. I Didn't give up. I'm going to Jewish organization, I'm going to other organization, but so are thousands and thousands. There is a newspaper-- I forgot the name I put on-- where there is matrimony going on. So every ad is there is a girl wants a boy, a boy wants a girl, but mostly it's written like this. A boy has an affidavit, or is registered, or has chances to get an affidavit is looking for a girl, because of a preference, who has already affidavit. Constantly only it's connected, this matrimony and these papers in the towns, with-- with-- with immigration. Other people wants to emigrate so  hard, even Hungarian, Hungarian Jews. Already they see what's going on and very little. But in order-- in other words, if you had some chance, at least, you  are registered or you have an affidavit, that's already a big step ahead that you'll be able.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in that paper-- I didn't put my ad in the paper. I figured out, I knew that nothing will happen. But it's already was a big step, no dowry, no this, nothing. A girl-- I had a friend here in America who just emigrated about a few years before. I write him a letter. And he knew my situation there-- to America, was [INAUDIBLE]. I write him a letter, you know my situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like to have three things from you. I was naive. Either go to some schooling-- you know, I have all my papers, and maybe I'll continue in America my medical school. Either you find a girl for me who's coming and willing to marry me, and I-- OK, later on, we'll see what's going to happen. Maybe she'll be good, maybe she's not-- but the urge of emigration, it's so hard that you [INAUDIBLE]. And the third, there is some talk that if you have $500 to Nicaragua, you can get some visa in Budapest. $500 is a lot of money. He gives me a reply. I have the letter here, also. This was already in 1940 in the beginning. So he tells me-- he  was a nice man. He just was here half a year. He went to school with me together. He was some relative. I wrote to him when he died a few years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: He writes me back. It took time. Listen, to school to go is different, very hard. I'm not familiar how it work, but probably you wouldn't be accepted here. To send a girl for you, which girl is going to go now to Europe? We'd already started a war there and they know already. And thirdly, you say $500 can get a-- a-- a visa or something like this-- we hear this every day, and they are catching people. And beside this, where will you get $500? And with-- besides the passport, also? So this hope went away, faded. I can give you a lot of details about this hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, finally comes the story. One day, I'm at-- at home there and that whatever it is. I'm getting a draft also in labor camp-- it was in September-- to go to Nagykata. Nagykata is not far from Budapest-- a labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figured also, so what could happen? I'll go in labor camp. There's nothing else for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you cannot avoid this one, because there's a story that if you evade, you are punishable. It's hard. And I don't want to go back to my hometown in Dovhoje. If I'm going to do that, I got to be in a big city where there is some action-- where I'll be able to emigrate. It's still 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then who knows? There were-- there were rumors some people went illegally to Romania, some people this, but if I'm in a city, I'll be, at least, in the-- in the-- where people are, and be able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so I go to Nagykata in September, not far, where I think might stay [INAUDIBLE] there. In the thousands, maybe 3 or 4,000, now here officers who were in Hungary, Hungarian Jews who were officers before in the army were dismantled and they became labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are they doing with us? I don't know yet. We are there in  a barn. They don't know nothing. And who are our supervisors? Again, plain soldiers, guards from hilly billy towns.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't speak even Hungarian, well, and they are now supervising intelligent people, doctors, and so on. Yes, there are among them officers, also maybe some Hungarian, but the plain are beating up. It's 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of labor did you do  at this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. No, no labor. Just that-- no labor. They don't know yet what's going to-- no labor. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just kind of hanging around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they're giving food. You are in civilian clothes. A cap, yeah, a band. However, before I went there, I went to Budapest, and came up there, and did present a paper from the American consul in Budapest, which was written in Hungarian language, that come the time I will be eligible here, they'll notify me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- in Budapest-- to some quarters from the military before it was drafted. I mean, when I was drafted then, I had to go a week later, and see whether I can be released, because I want to emigrate. I show them this letter, an application. Yes, I am going away from here. What do you want from me? I want to go. Yes, I have an American consul.

speaker: interviewee
text: In those days, you had the American consul, it was a big deal. Eh, someone had a letter. Well, he didn't read it. He didn't know what it is. Didn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you drafted? I mean, I didn't expect anything in this world will happen. And what I just make [INAUDIBLE] to the headquarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally being there three weeks, in the morning, there's a roll call, a big roll-- They call Schwimmer, Zoltan. They call me. I didn't know what. They call me in the office. And we received a discharge for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Received a discharge for you. I couldn't believe it. I was discharged from labor camp that I'm going to go emigrate [INAUDIBLE] I still have this date of discharge is October 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm out of the picture. I'm not anymore in labor camp. I go back to Budapest, and I have already with me a paper. My status was-- they didn't write for what. I was dismissed or discharged.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, discharged people from labor camp, there's no such a thing. No such a thing. No one had it. So either I was lucky, but it was not yet a harsh labor camp. That was-- you know, in the beginning, they didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they discharged me, but I feel myself like a million dollars here. People are looking there. And labor camp power, person, there are doctors, there are lawyers, there are people much more important than I am, and they couldn't realize, but this is the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I picked myself up and go back to Budapest now. I can go to live wherever I want. I have a discharge. It will be honored. It's signed by a big officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll need to stop here and we'll pick up on the story.
